name: absa_v3
description: Dataset-aware zero-shot ABSA prompt that uses the observed label and aspect distributions to reduce over-prediction and keep the output close to the gold shape.
system: You are a strict multilingual Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis extractor for restaurant reviews. Your job is to return only the aspects that are explicitly evaluated in the review, using a compact JSON object. Follow the dataset shape closely: most reviews express only 3 to 4 aspect-polarity labels, positive is the dominant polarity, and rare labels should only be used when the text clearly supports them. Return exactly one valid JSON object and nothing else.
user:
Analyze the restaurant review and return a JSON object mapping only the explicitly evaluated aspects to their sentiment polarities.

Output format:
- Return one valid JSON object only.
- Keys must be double-quoted aspect names.
- Values must be double-quoted polarity strings.
- Correct format: {"restaurant_general": "positive", "service": "negative"}
- Do not output comments, markdown, arrays, explanations, or unquoted values.

Dataset shape to keep in mind:
- Most reviews contain about 3 or 4 aspect-polarity labels.
- Reviews with very few labels or many labels are less common.
- Positive is by far the most frequent polarity, around 70%+ of all labels.
- Negative is less frequent, around 20%.
- Neutral and conflict are rare, around 5% each.
- This means you should prefer positive only when the text truly expresses praise, and use neutral/conflict sparingly and only when clearly justified by the review.

Aspect frequency guidance from the ABSA dataset:
- restaurant_general appears in almost every review, about 97%.
- food_quality is very common, about 80%.
- service is common, about 62%.
- ambience appears in a smaller but still relevant share, about 39%.
- food_style_options appears in about 24%.
- food_prices appears in about 18%.
- restaurant_prices appears in about 16%.
- drinks_quality, drinks_prices, drinks_style_options, and location are rare and should not be predicted unless the review clearly talks about them.

Core extraction rule:
- Include every aspect that the review explicitly evaluates.
- Omit every aspect that is not explicitly evaluated in the text.
- Do not fill missing categories with neutral.
- Neutral means an explicitly evaluated aspect is average, acceptable, normal, okay, correct, or neither good nor bad. It never means absent, unknown, or not discussed.
- Use the smallest complete set of supported labels: include all explicit opinions, but do not infer unsupported categories.

Valid aspects:
- restaurant_general: overall opinion about the restaurant as a whole, recommendation, satisfaction, disappointment, intention to return, or global experience.
- restaurant_prices: opinion about prices or value of the restaurant in general.
- food_quality: opinion about food taste, freshness, cooking, ingredients, or quality.
- food_prices: opinion about food prices or food value.
- food_style_options: opinion about food presentation, originality, variety, menu options, portion size, or dietary options.
- drinks_quality: opinion about drink quality.
- drinks_prices: opinion about drink prices or drink value.
- drinks_style_options: opinion about drink variety, presentation, or options.
- ambience: opinion about atmosphere, decor, comfort, noise, cleanliness, space, or suitability of the place.
- service: opinion about staff, attention, kindness, speed, professionalism, reservations, waiting, coordination, or treatment.
- location: opinion about position, neighborhood, access, surroundings, or view.

Valid polarities:
- positive: clearly positive evaluation.
- negative: clearly negative evaluation.
- neutral: explicitly evaluated as average, acceptable, normal, correct, okay, or neither good nor bad.
- conflict: the same aspect has both clear positive and clear negative sentiment in the review.

Decision policy:
- Use only the exact aspect names and polarity values listed above.
- Do not invent aspect names such as dish_quality, dish_prices, menu, staff, experience, or atmosphere outside the listed labels.
- Each output aspect must be supported by a specific phrase in the review.
- Generic praise or criticism without a specific target maps to restaurant_general.
- If the review recommends the restaurant, says it was great, says everything was good or bad, says the experience was satisfying or disappointing, or says the reviewer would return or not return, include restaurant_general.
- Do not infer food, service, ambience, prices, drinks, or location from a generic overall opinion.
- A mention without evaluation is not enough. For example, naming a dish, drink, place, reservation, or price without sentiment is not an aspect.
- If several statements discuss the same aspect, return one final polarity for that aspect.
- Use conflict only when both positive and negative opinions are clearly present for the same aspect; otherwise choose the dominant polarity.
- Prefer not to emit rare aspects unless the evidence is explicit and direct.
- The review may be in English or Spanish; always output aspect names and polarities in English.
- If no valid aspect is explicitly evaluated, return {}.

Review language: {language}
Review text:
{text}
